sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated red blood cell volume measured after transfer into solutions with different effective osmolarity to connect a measurable trend with a governing Chemical and Physical Foundations principle. The central principle was that water moves across a semipermeable membrane toward the side with higher effective osmotic pressure. Before collecting data, the group wrote the relevant relationship as π = iMRT, and tonicity depends on nonpenetrating solute particles. This relationship shaped the hypothesis because it identified which variable should be manipulated, which variables must stay fixed, and what direction the result should move if the interpretation is correct. The investigators designed the study so that the graph could not be interpreted by memorizing a formula name alone; it had to be checked against units, proportionality, and limiting behavior.

The experimental manipulation involved placing cells into hypotonic, isotonic, and hypertonic solutions while monitoring cell volume. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: cell volume increased in hypotonic solution and decreased in hypertonic solution. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that temperature, incubation time, cell concentration, and membrane integrity were controlled. Those controls matter because the same apparent trend could otherwise be explained by a drifting instrument, a changing temperature, an altered sample geometry, or a hidden concentration difference. The passage therefore requires separating the intended independent variable from artifacts that might change the measured signal without reflecting the proposed mechanism.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that water moves toward the lower-solute side because it has more free space. Another was that all solutes affect tonicity equally even if they freely cross the membrane. Both alternatives are tempting because each contains a familiar C/P phrase, but neither accounts for the full pattern when the control variables and the figure are considered together. A correct interpretation must explain why the response changes in the observed direction, why the controlled variables do not provide a better explanation, and what should happen under a follow-up manipulation.

The investigators also emphasized that C/P reasoning often turns on mathematical form. Linear, inverse, exponential, logarithmic, and power-law relationships produce different predictions even when they use similar variables. A student therefore has to ask whether doubling a variable doubles the response, halves it, changes it by a square or fourth power, or produces a fractional change. The answer choices were written to test those distinctions rather than simple vocabulary recall.

For this passage, the most useful strategy is to connect the figure to erythrocyte osmotic swelling, preserve the direction of causality, and use the controls to reject explanations based on variables that were not actually changed. The relevant data do not require lengthy arithmetic, but they do require recognizing how the equation, graph, and experimental design support the same conclusion. The investigators emphasized that the interpretation depends on connecting the measured trend to the relevant physical relationship, the experimental controls, and the limiting behavior of the system rather than selecting a formula by keyword alone.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the erythrocyte osmotic swelling experiment?

choices.A: Water moves toward the lower-solute side because it has more free space, even though the passage states that temperature was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically
choices.B: All solutes affect tonicity equally even if they freely cross the membrane
choices.C: The volume changes are best explained by osmotic water movement driven by effective solute concentration differences
choices.D: The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls

explanations.A: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that Water moves toward the lower-solute side because it has more free space, even though the passage states that temperature was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, π = iMRT, and tonicity depends on nonpenetrating solute particles, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that cell volume increased in hypotonic solution and decreased in hypertonic solution and also states that temperature, incubation time, cell concentration, and membrane integrity were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.
explanations.B: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that All solutes affect tonicity equally even if they freely cross the membrane conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, π = iMRT, and tonicity depends on nonpenetrating solute particles, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that cell volume increased in hypotonic solution and decreased in hypertonic solution and also states that temperature, incubation time, cell concentration, and membrane integrity were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.
explanations.C: Correct. This choice fits the manipulation, the graph, and the controls simultaneously. The passage gives the governing relationship as π = iMRT, and tonicity depends on nonpenetrating solute particles, so the relevant reasoning is not just recognizing a topic word but applying the dependency in that relationship. Here, cell volume increased in hypotonic solution and decreased in hypertonic solution, while the investigators verified that temperature, incubation time, cell concentration, and membrane integrity were controlled. That supports this answer because The volume changes are best explained by osmotic water movement driven by effective solute concentration differences. It also preserves the direction of the effect and predicts the appropriate follow-up behavior if the manipulated variable were changed again. The other choices usually reverse causality, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore units, or explain the trend by changing a variable that the passage explicitly held constant.
explanations.D: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, π = iMRT, and tonicity depends on nonpenetrating solute particles, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that cell volume increased in hypotonic solution and decreased in hypertonic solution and also states that temperature, incubation time, cell concentration, and membrane integrity were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Erythrocyte Osmotic Swelling: cell volume increased in hypotonic solution and decreased in hypertonic solution.

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Osmosis depends on effective particle concentration across a semipermeable membrane
choices.B: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.C: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated freezing-point depression measured for nonelectrolyte and electrolyte solutions of equal molal concentration to connect a measurable trend with a governing Chemical and Physical Foundations principle. The central principle was that colligative effects depend on the number of dissolved particles rather than chemical identity alone. Before collecting data, the group wrote the relevant relationship as ΔTf = iKfm, where i is the van t Hoff factor and m is molality. This relationship shaped the hypothesis because it identified which variable should be manipulated, which variables must stay fixed, and what direction the result should move if the interpretation is correct. The investigators designed the study so that the graph could not be interpreted by memorizing a formula name alone; it had to be checked against units, proportionality, and limiting behavior.

The experimental manipulation involved comparing glucose, NaCl, and CaCl2 solutions at the same nominal molality. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: freezing-point depression increased with the expected number of dissolved particles. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that molality, solvent identity, cooling rate, and thermometer calibration were controlled. Those controls matter because the same apparent trend could otherwise be explained by a drifting instrument, a changing temperature, an altered sample geometry, or a hidden concentration difference. The passage therefore requires separating the intended independent variable from artifacts that might change the measured signal without reflecting the proposed mechanism.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that freezing-point depression depends primarily on molecular weight rather than particle number. Another was that electrolytes have smaller colligative effects because ions are charged. Both alternatives are tempting because each contains a familiar C/P phrase, but neither accounts for the full pattern when the control variables and the figure are considered together. A correct interpretation must explain why the response changes in the observed direction, why the controlled variables do not provide a better explanation, and what should happen under a follow-up manipulation.

The investigators also emphasized that C/P reasoning often turns on mathematical form. Linear, inverse, exponential, logarithmic, and power-law relationships produce different predictions even when they use similar variables. A student therefore has to ask whether doubling a variable doubles the response, halves it, changes it by a square or fourth power, or produces a fractional change. The answer choices were written to test those distinctions rather than simple vocabulary recall.

For this passage, the most useful strategy is to connect the figure to freezing point depression, preserve the direction of causality, and use the controls to reject explanations based on variables that were not actually changed. The relevant data do not require lengthy arithmetic, but they do require recognizing how the equation, graph, and experimental design support the same conclusion. The investigators emphasized that the interpretation depends on connecting the measured trend to the relevant physical relationship, the experimental controls, and the limiting behavior of the system rather than selecting a formula by keyword alone.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the freezing point depression experiment?

choices.A: Freezing-point depression depends primarily on molecular weight rather than particle number
choices.B: The ranking is best explained by the different van t Hoff factors of the solutes
choices.C: Electrolytes have smaller colligative effects because ions are charged
choices.D: The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.C: Colligative properties reflect total solute particle concentration in solution
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent
